items.title: Do I need to get an agent or a publisher that is from the same region or country than me?
items.body:
I'm seriously considering going down the route of traditional publishing, getting an agent and publisher eventually, after I finish writing my book. However, my region is limited on those resorces (that I know of, at least) and honestly I'd prefer looking for options elsewhere.

I assume that many meetings with agents and publishers could happen online so, is it possible to get an agent or publisher from a different region or country? I know it might require some travelling but is it something agents or publishers could consider? My writing is in English, which already isn't my first language anyway. Would it be possible?

items.title: Can an author write in different writing styles in different books?
items.body:
I'm currently writing a story in the 1st person, a contemporary romance. However, I also have ideas for books that can be categorised in different genres (such as thriller). And for other ideas the book might be best if it is written in the 3rd person. Can one author vary the way they write depending on the book or genre?

I assume it is good that a writer has a peculiar writing style, something readers can realise, maybe see a patter, appreciate even perhaps. So maybe it would be ideal to keep the same writing style throughout books. But there are genres that could benefit from a different writing style or at least be written in a different person (3rd rather than 1st, for example). Should an author try to keep using the same style in all the books or is it okay to vary?

items.title: How can I make my blog more popular?
items.body: I write an action movie review blog where a review is published every Monday. How do I publicize my blog to bring more readers my way?

items.title: How do I say that someone is black?
items.body:
Sorry if this is an ancient solved problem to ask in 2019, but I googled about this and Quora isn't really answering me. I searched for the words black and race and politically as key-words on this website but still not a satisfactory answer.

The setting is 2013 in the UK, and my character is approaching three people two of them are sitting on a bench in a garden and one guy who is an elderly black man is standing.

As my character is approaching them, my novel reads;

I walked towards the trio who looked like they were having a conversation. They all seemed welcoming. A teenage looking girl and elderly women were sitting on the log and an elderly black man was standing in front of them.

I don't want to say it as black man and I am not sure of using the word African (will that clearly indicate without being broad). I want to express this just to mention the diversity in that environment although later on in my novel I have clearly expressed him in detail.

items.title: Do I need to clarify each chapter whose POV it is?
items.body:
I am currently writing a story in first-person, in which there are two different POVs, one for the main character and another for her love interest, a guy.

I explain when changing POV, usually in the beginning of the chapter. But sometimes I write multiple chapters in the same POV, only changing after some (maybe 3-4, it depends). Do I need to clarify in each chapter if the POV is the same as the last one or if it is the other character? I think, since it's only two POVs, and since they are rather different characters, that it is clear when it doesn't change. But I'm unsure.

What do you think?

items.title: What margins should I use for my book that I write in Word for binding and printing?
items.body: I am writing a book with no more than 120-150 pages, that I will send to a publisher in short. I have done all my writing in Word on my Mac, but I have problem with finding the right and proper margins for the settings and bookbinding. I hope someone here has the knowledge of knowing how to do with the margins and how to think. Is there any recommendations?

items.title: How to know when to end a chapter and start a new one?
items.body:
I am currently writing a story and I sometimes struggle with when to draw the line between one chapter and the next. I start to end the chapters when there is a moment of suspense, to keep the riders intrigued, or when there is a clear time skip or big change in the topic or location.

And yet, it is still something that confuses me sometimes. I'm a new author, still learning to improve my writing and trying to find out my style. Any advices on how to know when to separate chapters? Is there any good rule of thumb?

P.s. I saw that there was a similar question on the website but it was closed (someone claimed it was too vague) and I don't think it had any useful comments. Hoping someone with good insight can help.

items.title: What is the correct APA style for article author followed by a job title, when as a byline?
items.body:
I'm developing a style guide and we are identifying a situation where an article is written, followed by the job title of the article writer.

I understand that adding a job title after an author's name when in a paragraph structure should be like this: This article about national parks by John Smith, editor in chief, emphasizes the technology used throughout the many US parks.

The name, followed by the job title should be lowercase. However, this question is about the name, followed by a job title when used in a byline.

The Use of Technology in US National Parks
John Smith, Editor in Chief

The only thing I've been able to find thus far is regarding email signatures. Is that how the above should be written?

I'm trying to understand the APA way along with some documentation from an official APA site or some other official documentation so I have something to reference.

items.title: Can anyone suggest a publishing platform for me?
items.body:
For the past couple of years, my wife and I have had a killer mortgage, and I was trying to supplement my income with freelance writing. I wouldn't call it a total failure, there were some successes along the way that I am proud of. But as a reliable income generator, it just wasn't the way to go.

I wrote mainly for a platform that published quick little pop-culture pieces. I wrote about Star Trek and classic rock, because that's what I could churn out lots of quick articles about. And another publication that just did random general knowledge, that one was fun. But the most frustrating part was that doing something for money tends to suck the fun out of it.

Now, things have changed, I am financially stable for the first time in my life. No mortgage. At first I took a breather from writing, but now I realise that I can write for the pleasure and the passion of it.

So, I am hunting for a platform that will publish me. I do want to be paid, but I honestly do not mind if it's not much. A token amount so that I can keep calling myself a professional writer, and so that I don't feel like they'd just publish anyone (I'm a creative, my ego needs to be catered to). Something with not too much of an obligation, because I still need my day job.

Something preferably that publishes a wide variety of content, so that I'm not writing about the same topic over and over again.

And preferably somewhere established, where my work will be seen simply by dint of it being published there. If they're expecting me to bring the eyeballs, I can't do that. I quit social media for reasons I won't bore you with, so even as a semi-successful freelance writer, I never gained a following.

And somewhere that can see past my history. I have written so much about a sixty-year old TV show, one might get the impression it's all I can write about. And while I do have a lot of published pieces that aren't about Star Trek, if you look at it in terms of numbers, it looks like that's the comfort zone I can barely break out of. All I need is an open mind.

I know it sounds like I'm asking for a lot. Maybe too much. But I have hope because Listverse exists. Listverse ticks all the above boxes, and I have been published there quite a few times. It would be perfect if they weren't so prescriptive about the formats they publish (sometimes you don't want to do a listicle, sometimes you'd rather do a deep dive). But of course, Googling "sites like Listverse," I found way more listicle sites than I had imagined would exist.

I am very well-versed in pop-culture, but politics is somewhere I'd love to try and make a dent. I am politically active, and have a lot to say. So I'd love to write for somewhere that publishes opinion and think pieces.

Suggestions? Tips?

items.title: Question on a plot point
items.body: So there's this character and he's a super goofy, cheerful and let's say joyful. (side note, he's not dumb he's super smart) And he's the main character. But when he dies I want him to say "So this is what true joy feels like" because he finally found something that brought him "true joy" but I don't know how someone who's already very joyful would find "true joy"

items.title: How to write a Query letter?
items.body:
I'm currently writing my story, a few chapters away to the end. It will be the first of a series (with two books). I know there is still some time until I start sharing my story and writing queries but I would like to know more about it.

I know a query is to show possible agents or editors your book. I was told it was supposed to be a kind of summary of your story, a way to intrigue the person reading it. But how exactly should it be? What should I write and what should I avoid? How long should it be? Do I need to say something about myself or my writing in that query email?

Please lemme know. Thanks :)

items.title: How to describe a character dealing with a betrayal from one of their closest friends?
items.body:
The character who gets betrayed is my main character, who is practically left to die by her closest childhood friend. My MC is the type of character to wholeheartedly believes and trusts in her friends, so this betrayal comes as a huge shock to her. I want her to gradually lose trust in the people around her, including her other friends and family, and distance herself from them. For a while, she still cares about her friend who betrayed her, yet a part of her hates her deeply for betraying her and another one of her friends.

How can I describe this, while still not dragging it out too much, as I do have a rather strict word count to stick to?

items.title: How do I concatenate a string in Python?
items.body: I'm writing a Python 3.10+ script where I need to combine multiple string values, plus some numeric variables that I need to cast to strings, into one final output string. I know I can use the + operator for simple cases, like this: python 运行 greeting = "Hello" name = "Alice" message = greeting + ", " + name + "!" But this throws a TypeError if I try to append an integer directly without converting it first, and I've read that using + to join a lot of strings is inefficient because Python strings are immutable. I've also seen a bunch of other approaches mentioned in tutorials: str.join() for joining lists of strings f-strings (formatted string literals) str.format() method Old % style formatting io.StringIO for very large string builds What is the most idiomatic, Pythonic way to concatenate strings? Are there meaningful performance differences between these methods for different use cases? When should I pick one approach over the others? Are there any common gotchas I should avoid when building strings in Python? I don't need solutions for legacy Python 2, as our codebase is fully migrated to Python 3.

items.title: Capitalize and/or hyphenate "the Start Your Own Business project"?
items.body: I have come across a situation which drives me mad, and I cannot find any satisfying answer in the Oxford Guide to Style, which I tend to follow. The problem concerns the phrase "Start Your Own Business", and when it is used as the name of a project. I'm really confused about two things: capitalization and hyphenation. I've written it in capitals and without hyphens here, but that doesn't mean I prefer it that way. I think I would prefer it like this: the Start-Your-Own-Business project. Any ideas what is recommended here?

items.title: What POV type is better for a thriller book?
items.body:
I'm used to writing in 1st person writing, I like it more, it seems more personal. And I believe readers like it as well, to be immersed in the story.

And I know that I can switch POVs in the story, showing POVs from different characters.

My question is if I should keep with 1st person writing or change to 3rd person for a thriller. Since it is a genre that can include gore, violence and such other topics (TW) I'm not sure if it is okay to write it in 1st person, it might be overwhelming to some people. What do you think?

items.title: Descriptions, how long (or short) should it be?
items.body:
I know that it depends on a lot of factors, such as the author style, the scene, the chapter, amongst others. And I know how different readers might have different opinions. I know some might love description, pages and pages of details, while some might prefer for description to be kept to a minimum.

All that said, what is a good 'average' for description? How to know if I should add more description into my book or if more isn't necessary? And how to know when to describe in detail or when to keep it small or vague, maybe let the readers wonder on some details, letting them decide certain aspects how they invision it.

Any advice or feedback is welcome! Thanks :)

items.title: How many chapters should a romance story have?
items.body:
I saw online that a chapter in a romance story tends to be between 2500 and 5000 words. My chapters are within that length, some more towards 2000 or 3000 words, some over 4000 or might even be a bit longer than 5000 words. But mostly my chapters are between that length.

My question is, how many chapters should a romance book have? My book is the first of a saga (it should be a duo), so it will have more following it. Does the length of a book still apply to sagas? Or should books from sagas be shorter than standalone books? How many chapters or pages should romance books from sagas/series be?

items.title: Whats the best way to publish poetry in the Instagram age
items.body:
I write short poems about random things. Now my question is, what's the best and most profitable way to publish them in the age when people prefer social media and AI over books? Here are a few things I have considered:

  • Publishing a small book: but I'm not sure if it's profitable
  • Uploading them on my Instagram profile: but I've seen people post things written by others as their own, even going to the extent of declaring the original post as a copyright violation
  • Releasing a kindle version: though I'm not completely sure of how I'll bring traction to this
  • Giving them a tune and releasing as songs: I'm not sure if I have time to do that, or how to bring traction to my album if I release one

So, what's your take on this? What approach (or a combination of approaches) would you suggest for a person writing short poems?

items.title: Struggling with the placement of the final scenes of a novel as a discovery writer who writes out of order
items.body:
I write fiction as a discovery writer and I draft scenes out of order. I'm nearing the end of my first draft, but I'm struggling with one plot thread and with the buildup and escalation scenes of others. I find it challenging to judge scene placement and pacing without being able to view the manuscript as a whole.

Originally, my plan was to finish the entire first draft on paper before typing it into my computer. However, I'm considering typing the existing scenes up now and, as I do that, filling in the gaps so I can see the manuscript in order and identify where the remaining scenes should fit.

My concern is that this approach might lead me to start editing and revising instead of completing the first draft.

For writers who draft out of order (or anyone for that matter), do you think that typing up what I currently have of my manuscript will help me address my placement and structural issues and overcome my writer’s block? Or will it create more problems than it solves?

items.title: Do readers even like lyrical writing at all?
items.body:
I have attempted lyrical prose a number of times in my life, but I have often failed for lack of skill. And I was told that I have a problem with clarity. Although in recent days, I seem to struggle with it less, I, for lack of skill, still have trouble achieving asthetically pleasing rhythm. So I can understand if people don't like writings of mine in which I attempted a lyrical sound, though I seldom post anything, or even write anything. But knowing, as I've been told, that many readers dislike complexity of language, I wonder if people even like lyrical language at all.

I want to write lyrical language, and I want such writing of mine to delighted in. Nevertheless, it appears that such writing is not prefered by many, though I'm not sure if I've actually been told this. My question is, "Do people even like lyrical writing at all, writing in which scansion is employed for effect?" Thank you for your answers.

items.title: How to write a character that's morally bad?
items.body: Is making them ignorant towards it a good idea? Or making them relatable; does giving them characteristics that people can relate to make them well-written? I'm trying to avoid whatever the hell Jax was in The Amazing Digital Circus - I don't want someone as unlikeable as Jax, but then again I want them to be someone you can hate as much as Jimmy from Mouthwashing? Does that make sense? I absolutely despise Jimmy, but I understand how he is a well-written character, but for someone like Jax I just see them as annoying?

items.title: Any tips for writing a battle scene that is more heard than seen?
items.body:
Here are the details. I want to write a character witnessing this battle, but more so by hearing it than watching it. It is a sort of medieval-inspired setting, so there will be plenty of bows and swords and whatnot. It is also fantasy, so there will probably also be things like unnatural smoke/fog/shadow, etc. Other than that, it takes place mostly in a forested location (there can be some out in the open if need be, but for the POV character, it should definitely be at least partially obscured).

The character is not part of this battle, at least not in a traditional sense. From what I'm thinking right now, she starts out in a place where she can hear and maybe see some of what's going on, but she wants a better view. She climbs to a high watchtower, but even there the view is heavily obscured by unnatural shadow, etc.

For the first part of this battle, the "good guys" are being bested. But then there's a magical occurrence that turns things around, and they're able to chase their opponents off.

I hope that's enough context to give the picture for what I'm wanting to do. Any specific advice or suggestions for how to make this work? I'll also take tips on how to create the right sort ambience for this scene. References to any helpful sources are also appreciated.

Please no broadly general advice like "figure out what the goal of the scene is" or "identify the character's motivation," etc. I already know about these principles, but they're separate from what I'm focusing on in this question.

items.title: How many main characters should a novel have?
items.body: How many main characters (protagonists) is it appropriate for a novel to have? Would five or six main characters be too many?

items.title: How can I find reputable literary agents who accept free submissions?
items.body: I am a new writer currently working on my first novel and I feel completely lost about the publishing side of things. I have finished a rough draft of my manuscript and I am now looking for guidance on the next steps. Can anyone here tell me where and how I can find reputable literary agents who accept submissions from debut authors for free and online? I do not have money to pay for services and I want to avoid scams. What websites, databases, or directories are safe to use for agent research? Also, what is the standard process for submitting a query letter and manuscript online? Do most agents use QueryManager, email, or their own portals, and are there any specific formatting rules I should know before I send anything out? I would really appreciate detailed advice from writers who have gone through this process before.

items.title: Can I change POV in the middle of a chapter?
items.body:
I am currently writing a romance story and I try to keep the same POV for a while. My story has 2 POVs, the main character's and the one of her male love interest. So far I have written a few chapters together in one POV (Point of View) before changing.

I try to at least keep the same POV throughout the same chapter. But today I started writing another chapter and it would be better to change POV in the middle of the chapter, to see the event through other pair of eyes. Can I change it or is it better not to?

Please lemme know. Thanks!

items.title: How long should chapters be? Shorter or longer?
items.body: I never know when to stop a chapter, how long it should be. Right now my chapters are on the smaller side, I try to end in an interesting moment to keep the reader intrigued and hooked. But I never know if they are too short or not. Do people prefer shorter chapters (a few pages, less than 10) or longer ones? Or does it depend on the type of chapter? Please lemme know.

items.title: How to know if my story is considered a slow burn?
items.body:
I'm currently writing a romance story and I think I can categorise it as a 'slow burn' but I'm not sure. How 'slow' does the romance between the characters have to be to fall in that category?

For context, I have written about 1/3 of the story (currently writing chapter 20 I believe) and they are just now getting more comfortable, more familiar, with still some awkward moments and tension. They have only hugged once (spontaneous, out of happiness, after good news), nothing more. Maybe brushed fingers once, not sure, but that is it. Can I consider it 'slow burn'?

Thanks!

items.title: How can I fit a protagonist and his character arc to an existing world and plot?
items.body:
I began my project with world-building. Afterward, I decided on the overarching plot, and now I'm trying to populate it with characters, starting with my protagonist. I've figured out his core driving force, but I have trouble coming up with a growth arc for him.

Plot & Premise

My world is a classic fantasy mixed with cosmic horror. The evil wizard messes with forces beyond his comprehension, leading to his undoing before he can rise to power, so the protagonist gets to grow up in a peaceful world. (Think Harry Potter if Tom Riddle got himself killed before he could become Lord Voldemort.)

But because the two are connected by fate, whatever happened to the wizard has left a stain on the hero's soul, too. I haven't worked out the details—it may be that something's wrong with his magic, or he wades through life discontent, feeling like he's missing some fundamental piece of himself—but it's going to be the force that drives him down the same road as the wizard, in search for answers.

The turning point will be him accidentally unleashing what has become of the wizard upon the world. (Rather than a person, the wizard will have taken an intangible form and will spread like a virus. It can only be tracked through its effects on people, like in Bird Box.) He has to fix his mistake and defeat the horror before it destroys everything.

Problem

I'm struggling to come up with a character arc for the protagonist. The standard method is to give him some kind of flaw which he eventually overcomes in the climax. Alternatively, as described here, a past experience will have given rise to a harmful belief, which the character plasters over with another belief, and he must realize he's lying to himself and muster the courage to move past it; or something like that.

I don't know how to fit my character into that framework. His problems stem from an external source. No amount of internal growth will fix that. Do I need to give him another problem, one which, upon confronting and overcoming, will allow him to fix the external problem?

I should note that the cosmic horror is intended as a background element to enrich the world, like Tolkien did with his distant mountains. It is the theme of the story—fear of the unknown—and I built the world and the magic system around it, but the main conflict is in ethnic tensions (which spring from the same fear). My other great woe is trying to weave the two conflicts, the hero's exploration of the eldritch corners of the magical world and his position in the middle of said tensions, into a cohesive plot.

items.title: Is my story idea original enough?
items.body: The story follows 7 people who each represent a sin, and it explains how their sin affects their life and status. The power comes from an unknown man who is trying to destroy destiny itself. The main villain is the one who gives to the seven people. The main villain's goal is to hire 7 people who rebelled against destiny, and the main cast don't know that they are helping the main villain, thinking that they are doing the right thing.

items.title: Will publishing on Kindle, or making Audio Books, hurt my profits in the long run?
items.body:
Should a self-publisher stick to just "hard copy" print-on-demand publishing of his books? Would adding Kindle, or hiring an "audio book" company, compete too much with the hard copy editions?

Hard copies sell for more money than either of the other two. But would the demand for the hard copy be less, so the financial bottom line is less? Would more hits on Kindle make up for revenue lost on less demand for the the hard copies?

items.title: Is social commentary a requirement for sci-fi?
items.body: I mean most things you will ever write regardless of genre will have some degree of social commentary to them... but I feel like this is something that's way more common in sci-fi. But is it a strict requirement to have some profound remark on society to be able to write something meaningful in the genre? I think I've phrased this in a really broad way but I hope what I've specified is enough to answer my question...

items.title: How to deal with seriously ill characters and what type of illness should I give them
items.body:
My heroine is seriously ill with something that can sooner or later take her life away at any moment. She takes medication regularly but refuses to undergo anything hardcore (like surgery or chemo or anything that would make her better or cure her for good). I have not figured out what or where the kind of illness she has yet since i am not a certified doctor with enough knowledge about the human body but i do want to be specific when it comes to symptoms or medication and its side effects.

I also don't know how much that illness should effect her daily life. She's an intelligent teacher with exceptional understanding and memory. She is swamped with work and responsibilities that don't often involve her career as a teacher but it also greatly affects her health.

She's rich, so money isn't an issue. She just thinks that she should live her life to the most instead of undergoing harsh treatments and so.

Is there any type of illness (that preferably includes attacks such as asthma) that hinders someone's day to day life but not as much as staying to bed all day and is curable under extreme medication? (Cancer is out since she has to work everyday for being a teacher and other tiring stuff)

items.title: How should writers interpret “ambiguous” citation verification results?
items.body:
I’m working with long-form non-academic writing (blog posts, reports, AI-assisted drafts) where citations are not always perfectly formatted.

Some citation-checking workflows classify sources into categories such as:

  • verified (clear match),
  • ambiguous (partial or unclear match),
  • or unverifiable.

My question is specifically about handling the “ambiguous” case.

When a citation partially matches a real source but cannot be conclusively verified:

  • Is it acceptable to keep it with additional manual checking?
  • Should it be rewritten or replaced with a clearer source?
  • At what point does ambiguity make a citation unsuitable for publication?

I’m looking for best-practice guidance from experienced writers and editors, rather than tool-specific advice.

items.title: Where do prose rhythms come from?
items.body:
Do you think that the rhythms of poetic prose compositions are produced by their author with a sense of rhythm in mind, or by tinkering with their wording until they arrive at a rhythm they like?

I am asking because after wanting to emulate the prose rhythm of the KJV Bible for several years, when I was about 21 years old I wrote a five paragraph piece with perfect prose rhythm, whose rhythm was not an imitation of anything I ever read before. And I wonder how the rhythms of the first poetic prose compositions were formed.

I am talking about any kind of prose in a style above the plain style, such as the Bible, certain kinds of speeches, certain kinds of fictional works, etc.

Where do prose rhythms actually come from? Are they learned by imitation and discovery? Or are they formed by the imagination with little effort?

This question may seem off-topic, but it is actually a practical question of how to write with rhythm.

items.title: Is frequent releasing of new books a good alternative to doing promotions?
items.body:
Is it true that releasing several well-written books consecutively/frequently will lead to sales without any need to do promotions?

I have heard some indie-publishing authors/pundits say that a better (or alternative) way to push your books and have high sales is simply by releasing books frequently, and promotion is thus unnecessary. One particular strong advocate of this notion is an author called Dean W. Smith. In fact, he claims that the need for marketing/promotions is "a myth," and that all you have do is write and publish frequently and your books will promote themselves as people find them.

I would like to know if this is true, because I notice that the man has never provided any actual evidence to support his assertion. Does merely writing and releasing (good) books frequently work? And if so, why would that be? As i understand it, when a book is published on Amazon by an unknown author without doing any promotion of it, it merely sits there and sinks into the ocean and it will never get sold. Why would it make any difference if you add more books in the same way? Wouldn't they all just sink into the abyss?

Or perhaps what they mean is that doing so simply increases your odds of one of your books being accidentally seen by a customer (just like buying many lottery tickets increases your chances at winning a lottery). Is that simply what they mean? Because they never actually elaborate on how exactly they are claiming it works.

items.title: Can some please clarify the rules of stress for me?
items.body:
I was having trouble determining which words are stresed.

How do you tell the words that are stressed from the words that are unstressed?

Thank you.

items.title: How to come up with an original super power?
items.body: This is for a comic and the power must be original and interesting.

items.title: How do I show character development in a subtle and natural way?
items.body: I'm writing a 4-6 book series, with a female in her late 20s as the protagonist. It's in first person, and I'm struggling to think of how to show her development without having to spell it out and it seeming unrealistic. She's starting out as shy with low self esteem, a bit self-involved. I don't want her to end up perfect as I want it to be realistic, and I'm struggling with how to show the development subtly over time.

items.title: Dramatic Irony but when one character knows more than another character
items.body: Dramatic irony is when the audience knows more than the character. But what is it called when character 2 knows more than character 1? and doesn't reveal that information (to the audience either) until near the end?

items.title: How to write a scene with sword training (2 people) supervised by a 3rd person?
items.body:
I want to have a scene that's not passive, but it's also not a dramatic, life-or-death, high stakes sword fight. It's just training or practice. The students are two young people, and are supervised by a 3rd person. This 3rd person has been their unofficial trainer or instructor, but due to recent injuries, can't actively participate himself. The students are advanced enough that he can instruct or supervise from the sidelines. So the scene will have dialogue in it, too.

Again, I want it to be a scene where there's some action, but not drama. And I want the activity to be believable/realistic for young trainees. Does anyone have advice or sources I can look at that might be helpful for this?

items.title: How to write cinematic romance?
items.body: I feel like my writing doesn't have a mood to it and its really plain jane most of the times, and I want the mood to be sensory/cinematic, think sweather weather, lana del ray, 3am, motel signs, headlights, empty roads. Not sure how to achieve that feeling of sadness/hopelessness but also incorporate into romance at the same time when it's just a normal love story with no dark backstory. Settings? Dialogue? Plot?

items.title: Can I start an Opening Line with Dialogue if writing in third person (limited or omniscient)?
items.body: Can I start an Opening Line with Dialogue if writing in third person (limited or omniscient)? Yes or no?

items.title: Is there any method to identify the type of an essay?
items.body:
I am preparing for an essay exam. The essay topics on which students are usually asked to write in that exam range from politics and governance to philosophical and ethical issues. My essay teacher has instructed us that before you should start writing an essay, first one should identify the type of that essay. He told us that there are three types of essay--argumentative, persuasive, and expository--and the other types, such as abstract and discursive essays, are subtypes of these three types of essay. Although he has guided us a bit on how to identify the type of an essay, I am still having difficulty.

The exam is 3 hours long and we can't spend too much time on just identifying the category a essay falls in.

Following are some of the statements from the past papers:

  • Artificial Intelligence: The Death of Creativity.
  • Pragmatism vs Passion in politics.
  • Instruction in youth is like engraving in stone.

items.title: Hide or Show Villains' (Main Antagonist's) Name in Prologue? Which One?
items.body: For introducing a villain (The Main Antagonist) in the prologue, should his/her name be revealed for who he/her is for the events of the story to come? Or keep the villain's name a mystery and use minimal descriptions of his/her appearance in the shadows and show a sample of their heinous actions to be revealed later on?

items.title: How long does it take to publish in a magazine?
items.body:
How long does it take to publish in a magazine? I have had a piece under consideration—with a fair number of emails and discussions with the editor—for five months with the editor still on the fence about accepting it. I am planning to re-inquire about its status (it's been a month since the last one). Is this an exceptionally long time for a magazine submission?

For additional context, the magazine is semi-academic in nature. It is a theological publication and very much so not peer-reviewed, though the editorial board does take an active interest in making sure it's "up to snuff," which my paper had gone through in October.

A Post Script: Thank you everyone! Based on what you've said, I decided to reach back out to the editor. He decided to come back with a rejection, though he has given me useful feedback over this whole 5 month process. Unfortunate, but not fruitless. Either way, a good learning experience. Thank you, again!

other_site.audience: professional linguists and others with an interest in linguistic research and theory
other_site.name: Linguistics

items.title: Is it possible to adapt ancient graeco-roman prosodic styles, forms, principles, modifications into modern verses?
items.body:
Is it possible to adapt ancient graeco-roman prosodic styles, forms, principles, modifications into modern verses?

Does anybody know good authors who write in vernacular or modern languages with greek iambic trimetre and using prosodic rules in Greek tragedy or other kinds of plays or poetry? is there a way for it to succeed or is it more about trial and fails? Name for prosodic pattern in which the last line in a verse is much shorter than all the rest for example is said to be sapphic with an adonic final line. Or this lyrical poem of Nietzsche's in iambic trimetre which I assume he models after Greek tragedy's metre and has catalexis and metre substitutions. (Do his lyrics follow or resemble any ancient Greek prosodic styles?) What are the major ancient Greek prosodic styles, rules, modifications that are possible to be adapted into modern verses?

items.title: Content Patterns
items.body:
In a small book I read on rhetoric, the author instructs readers to learn rhetorical forms, which seem in my understanding patterns of content. One example of such a pattern, which is given in the book, is a paragraph pattern containing four elements: claim, reason, illustration, claim.

I assume that, more generally, and in many genres, there are numerous patterns such as this, not only at the paragraph level, but at higher levels as well. Likely, there are many kinds of paragraphs, and many ways of structuring content at higher levels, existing in literature. I want to know what all those patterns are, and where to learn them.

So this is my question: Where do I find a treatment of all the patterns of content existing in literature? Are there books I can read, or websites I can visit, that teach them?

items.title: How to come up with a good seed for a main character?
items.body:
I'm currently in the midst of creating a fantasy series, and have created a character with hearing loss (inspired by my own hearing loss). I noticed that almost every successful story begins with a seed, or a simple/mundane innate desire of the main character or something that happens to them. For example, Tolkien's writing started off with the seed being what would happen if a Hobbit's ordinary life was disrupted.

The crux is that I have settled on having my MC have hearing loss and that being something he works around to find his place in a society not friendly towards hearing, but I don't know how to make his story unique and not just another Daredevil or Toph Beifong. How should I proceed?

items.title: How to properly cite a reuploaded documentary?
items.body:
YouTube channel ‘Mental Health Treatment’ reuploaded a documentary on duck imprinting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2xd7o3z957c

The title just says ‘Vintage psychology study of imprinting in ducks. Behaviorism, duck science’.

No source is given and the clip doesn’t have credits. I’ve run Google searches for several quotes from the clip but I can’t find the original source.

How would I cite this clip? It doesn’t seem right crediting ‘Mental Health Treatment’ for the clip since it isn’t theirs. I’m not even sure they own the rights to the clip. But it does have useful information I want to cite and that I can’t easily find elsewhere.

other_site.audience: writers/artists using science, geography and culture to construct imaginary worlds and settings
other_site.name: Worldbuilding

items.title: What is the point of making a timeline for your world?
items.body:
Even if some major events of your world do have relevancy, I believe making an entire timeline is a bit too far, considering that you have to add all of those thousands of years of countless events to your plotline at some point, and perhaps connect it to other details of your own worldbuilding. I'm pretty sure you need to have expanded quite a lot in your worldbuilding to be able to do such a thing.

Is there a reason to go to all that effort?

items.title: APA in-text citation of multiple works by the same author in the same year
items.body: For the APA 7th edition style, I am citing several numbers from the same overall website and author (but different links to each number) in the same line. How does the in-text citation look like? Based on what I have seen online, would the citation be as such: (Author, 2025a,b,c,d)

items.title: Can I have a non-living thing with its own perspective?
items.body: We typically have third person or first person narratives in literature. I have a requirement - to unveil the suspense, I want a non-living thing to share its perspective in the final chapter. Is this permissible, justified and/or sensible?

items.title: Capitalizing within a sentence
items.body:
I am writing dialogue. I have googled this to no avail.

Jane said, "What is your vision statement?"
Mike replied, "It's 'To make men of men.'"

Should "To" be capitalized, and are the single quotes correct?

items.title: If my story is set in China but written in English, should I use Chinese names or English ones?
items.body: If my story is set in China but written in English, should I use Chinese names or English ones? Because in almost every book I read, the characters have Westernized or mixed Asian-Western names.

items.title: Writing Rigid or Nostalgic Characters?
items.body:
I've been trying to make a character that longs for a return to normalcy after entering "ACT II" Context: [He expresses discontent regarding moving and his sister leaving for college, believing his normal life is crumbling.]

I have the characterization down, but I fail to find how to incorporate these character flaws into the story to inspire a character arc of adaptation (especially since it's in the paranormal mystery genre). How can I write a character arc for someone who resists or hesitates towards the crazy events and changes of a story?

Additionally, what could be done to create a believable reversal situation where the character, upon adapting to the "ACT II situation," doesn't want to solve the mystery, knowing that would resume the changes (moving out and sister leaving) of the normal world?

P.S.: This character was inspired by Bilbo Baggins, so something like him.

items.title: Is there a way to publish a singular short story?
items.body: I've written a short story and I've been wondering whether I could somehow share it somewhere, but I think any method to publish it would require a set of short stories to make a sort of anthology or collection... But I only have one... What are some possible avenues for people in this situation? I've spent a while on this and I'd like to put it somewhere instead of just shelving it and moving on.

items.title: Tagging dialogue lines with character names using hotkeys
items.body:
Is there a program/tool that can use to deal with boilerplate such as indicating who is speaking in my text on the go with hotkeys? What I'm imagining is, for example, when writing a back-and-forth dialogue I can just select a text segment and press a preconfigured key dedicated to a character and the line gets "assigned" to that character in some way. This can be highlighting the text a specific color, or a literal auto rearranging of the text so it looks like {CharacterName}: "text". That way I don't have to spend a bunch of time constantly typing up character names or punctuation and keep the flow of my writing.

This becomes an issue at times when I get in the flow of things, end up with a couple thousand words of text and then have to untangle who said what for a while.

I mostly use Scrivener but I'm also open for using other tools/word processors.

items.title: How do you write in an optimistic voice?
items.body:
I am extremely comfortable with writing in a cynical or pessimistic voice, and I've realised it often becomes my default when writing. But I want to expand my range to be able to write in the perspective of characters with different outlooks on life. The issue is I try to write a more optimistic character, I end up making them sound too cheerful and they start speaking in shallow platitudes, which makes them unbearable to read.

I have read books with more optimistic characters, but I can't emulate the natural optimism in their voice. What is a way for me to work on this?

items.title: Handwriting speed
items.body:
Currently I am 22 years old. I have a very bad handwriting. Also, I kinda feel I write slow when compared to other students of my age.

Back in 10th grade, I was so slow that I was unable to finish my exam within the stipulated time despite knowing the answers. I was able to complete writing only for around 90% of the total marks in some exams. I ended up getting bad grades not because I didn't study but because I was unable to complete all the questions due to lack of time.

My writing speed increased however the legibility remained the same. Also, when I try to write fast in exams, I get pain in my arms and sometimes fingers too after an hour or two which in turn reduces my speed.

I tried to calculate my writing speed. I wrote the following in 2 minutes.

1st image corresponds to my fastest writing. I wrote 288 characters in 2 minutes, which means the speed of my fastest writing is 144 characters per minute.

2nd image corresponds to my neatest writing. I wrote 112 characters in 2 minutes, which means the speed of my neatest writing is 56 characters per minute.

I have a few questions:

  1. Is my handwriting speed slower than normal?

  2. If it is slow, is there a way to improve the legibility of my fastest handwriting so that I can write faster?

items.title: How long is "too long" to write something?
items.body:
I've been writing a short story for almost a year now. It's about 2400 words long. I've edited it multiple times because I have struggled quite a lot with firstly getting the first draft written out, and then also editing it multiple times over the course of the year - while also balancing other non-writing responsibilities. So I was wondering whether that pace is "too slow" and if it's maybe time to put it aside to write something else?

Only issue I have with that is I have a very clear idea on what I want to write with that short story, and I do enjoy writing it. However, I can't seem to write anything else until this one is done because I constantly think about that one. I've also realised that it's a large time investment, and I'm wondering whether it's worth trying something else now...

For anyone who's experienced this, what did you ultimately end up doing with your story?
